article_title: I Tried Tainer for Proxmox and It Feels Like Portainer for VMs
core_thesis: Tainer is a self-hosted, Docker-deployable management layer for Proxmox that provides a modern, Portainer-like UI for managing VMs and containers, abstracting the default Proxmox interface to simplify workflows in home labs and small environments, though it is still early-stage with limited advanced features.
detailed_summary: The article introduces Tainer, a new open-source tool that acts as a management layer on top of Proxmox, interacting via the Proxmox API to offer a cleaner, more modern interface. The author compares it to Portainer for Docker, emphasizing its focus on simplifying VM and container management tasks. Tainer was initially a SaaS product but now offers a self-hosted version deployable via Docker or Docker Compose, giving users full control over their data. The deployment process is straightforward: a simple Docker run command or a Docker Compose file with environment variables for SMTP and APP_URL. After deployment, users create an admin account and connect their Proxmox cluster by providing API URL, credentials, and node details. The dashboard displays key performance metrics (CPU, memory, storage, network). Features include deployment creation from templates or scratch, backup health dashboards, alerting (high CPU/memory/disk, storage issues, stale backups), node configuration snapshots (still in development), a CVE scanner with debscan, a load balancer (not ProxLB), template management with full VM/container settings, image management (Docker Hub search), and ISO management with URL downloads. The author notes pros: simple modern UI, quick Docker deployment, easy visibility, minimal config, good for small environments, unique features like node snapshots. Cons: limited advanced features, some functionality incomplete, smaller community, few integrations, not ideal for large scale, lacks deep monitoring. The article concludes that Tainer is not a full Proxmox UI replacement but a great supplementary interface, especially for mobile management, and recommends it for home lab enthusiasts.

entities.name: Tainer
entities.role: Software project providing a modern management UI for Proxmox, deployable via Docker.

entities.name: Proxmox
entities.role: Open-source virtualization platform that Tainer manages via its API.

entities.name: Portainer
entities.role: Docker management UI that inspired Tainer's design philosophy.

entities.name: Brandon Lee
entities.role: Author and senior writer at Virtualization Howto, testing and reviewing Tainer.

entities.name: Docker
entities.role: Container platform used to deploy Tainer.

key_data_points: Tainer is a self-hosted management layer for Proxmox that provides a modern, Portainer-like UI for VMs and containers, deployable via Docker or Docker Compose. (🟢 Relevant to your homelab/DevOps: easy to spin up in your existing Docker environment for centralized Proxmox management.)

Deployment requires a simple Docker run command: `docker run -d --name tainer --restart unless-stopped -p 3000:3000 -v tainer-data:/app/data tainersh/tainer:latest`. (🟢 Practical for quick homelab setup; you can integrate into your Docker Compose stack.)
Tainer's dashboard shows key performance metrics (CPU, memory, storage, network) for Proxmox cluster nodes. (🟢 Useful for homelab monitoring at a glance.)

The tool includes a CVE scanner with debscan for security vulnerabilities in hosts, VMs, and containers. (🟢 Relevant to your Red Teaming: can be used to identify vulnerable targets in your lab for offensive exercises.)
Tainer offers alerting for high CPU/memory/disk, storage unhealthy/low space, stale backups, and node high CPU/memory. (🟢 Helps automate monitoring in your homelab.)
Node Configs feature allows taking snapshots of Proxmox host configuration, but is still in development. (🟢 Potential for configuration backup automation once mature.)
Tainer includes a load balancer (not ProxLB) to distribute workloads across Proxmox nodes. (🟢 Useful for homelab resource optimization.)

The tool is best suited for small environments and home labs; it lacks advanced features and deep monitoring for large-scale setups. (🟢 Manage expectations: good for learning and small deployments, not production.)
methodology_steps: Deploy Tainer using Docker: run `docker run -d --name tainer --restart unless-stopped -p 3000:3000 -v tainer-data:/app/data tainersh/tainer:latest`.

Alternatively, use Docker Compose: create a `docker-compose.yml` with the tainer image, port mapping, volume mount, and environment variables (APP_URL, SMTP settings). Then run `docker compose up -d`.
After deployment, access the web UI at the container host's IP on port 3000. Create the first admin account.
Connect your Proxmox cluster by providing the API URL, username, password, default node, and TLS verification settings. Click 'Initialize workspace' to see the dashboard.

Explore features: dashboard metrics, deployments (create VMs/containers from templates), backups dashboard, alerts configuration, node config snapshots, CVE scanner, load balancer, template management, image management (Docker Hub), and ISO management.
conclusions: Tainer provides a modern, mobile-friendly UI for Proxmox that simplifies VM and container management, making it a valuable addition to a home lab environment. The author recommends it for users seeking a cleaner interface than the default Proxmox UI, especially for quick deployments and monitoring.

The tool is still early-stage: some features (like node config snapshots) are incomplete, and it lacks advanced capabilities for large-scale environments. However, its ease of deployment via Docker and focus on abstraction make it a promising project for homelab enthusiasts.

For Red Teaming, the built-in CVE scanner can help identify vulnerable VMs/containers in your lab, allowing you to practice exploitation in a controlled environment. The alerting system also aids in maintaining lab uptime for continuous testing.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is directly relevant to your homelab and DevOps interests, as Tainer offers a Docker-deployable, modern UI for managing Proxmox VMs and containers—similar to Portainer. You can quickly integrate it into your existing Docker stack to simplify Proxmox management, monitor performance, and automate alerts. The CVE scanner is a bonus for your Red Teaming practice, letting you identify and exploit vulnerabilities in your lab. As a student, the tool's simplicity helps you focus on learning virtualization concepts without wrestling with the default Proxmox interface. The article also provides concrete deployment steps and a balanced pros/cons list, helping you decide if it fits your setup.
extra_advice: Consider deploying Tainer in a Docker container on a dedicated management VM or a lightweight host (e.g., a Raspberry Pi or low-power mini PC) to keep it separate from your Proxmox nodes. Use the CVE scanner to periodically audit your lab VMs and containers, then practice exploiting the discovered vulnerabilities with tools like Metasploit or custom scripts. This turns Tainer from a simple management UI into a component of your Red Team lab infrastructure.
